lines.text: Good evening, Madison. It's {{CLOCK}}, and if you're hearing this, the Purge is already underway.
We went live at 19:00 sharp. The clock started. The law stopped. And here we are.
I'm Quinn Foley. You're listening to PRGE — Purge Radio — and I want to welcome every single person who decided to stay home tonight.
We're {{ELAPSED}} in, and so far, the night is holding steady. Not quiet — steady.
Before we get into the details, I need to tell you something about the broadcast tonight: we're experiencing some signal interference.
Quinn, that's my cue. Tim Peplinski here, station ops. We've got a jammer somewhere in the north sector, approximately 200 meters from our rig.
It's intermittent. Not strong enough to take us off the air, but you might notice the signal cutting in and out. We're working the antenna.
If you lose us, stay tuned. We'll be back. This has happened before. We're still here.
There it is. Technical difficulties are part of the package. Welcome to community radio during the only night that matters.
Caroline, I want to know what the weather is going to do to us tonight. Give me the full picture.
I've been tracking the systems since yesterday. Right now, we're looking at clear skies, 41 degrees, light southwest winds at 8 miles per hour.
Visibility is excellent — 10 miles or better. Perfect conditions for... well, for whatever's about to happen out there.
But here's what matters: there's a cold front moving in from the northwest, and it's going to arrive around midnight, give or take.
When it hits, temperatures drop fast. We're talking low 30s by 01:00. Wind gusts could reach 20 miles per hour.
Rain's going to turn to sleet. Visibility drops to 4 miles. That's the story for hours 5 through 8 of the broadcast.
So if anyone out there is thinking about being outside tonight, they need to know: it gets hostile up there.
Noted. Cold front at midnight. Wind and sleet for the back half. Anyone out there, dress warm. Not that you should be out there.
Holden, you've been quiet. What do the numbers tell us before we even get started?
Looking at pre-Purge trends. Historical comparison to this exact time last year shows us at a baseline entry point.
2168, Hour 1 ended with 8 confirmed kills across Madison. We're tracking right now for similar numbers, maybe slightly elevated.
East Side is where we expect to see the first spike. It's the neighborhood with the lowest security density and historically the earliest activity window.
Capitol Square will be fortified. West Side wealthy enclaves have private security response protocols. South Side's been investing in community barricades.
But East Side — Atwood, Schenk's Corners — they're going to light up fast. That's where our attention should be in the next 2 hours.
So we expect the familiar pattern. The neighborhoods that can defend themselves will. The ones that can't... we'll hear about it.
Precisely. And one more data point: Lake Mendota. Water temperature tonight is approximately 38 degrees, down from the seasonal average.
If anyone's considering the water as an escape route, survivability window at that temperature is 15 to 20 minutes. Just as a fact.
That's cheerful. Thank you, Holden. That's the kind of thing that keeps you up at night — literally, for the next 12 hours.
I'm getting a signal fluctuation on the west antenna. Adjusting now.
And there's the interference again. You hearing that? That's the jammer reminding us we're still a fugitive operation.
For anyone new to PRGE: we operate exactly one night a year. The rest of the year, this frequency is illegal. Tonight, everything is legal.
But someone doesn't want us broadcasting. That's a compliment, actually. Means we matter.
Quinn, I'm seeing the wind shift earlier than my model predicted. Southwest to west, more aggressive than the forecast indicated.
The front could accelerate. Could be 23:00 instead of midnight. I'm going to be monitoring.
Caroline tracking the atmospheric drama while we track the other kind. That's the split screen tonight.
Speaking of which, I pulled the neighborhood-watch data from last year. Williamson Street mounted an armed barricade at the Yahara River bridge.
They held for 90 minutes without breaches. Zero kills inside the perimeter. That's a model worth watching tonight.
If we see that strategy replicated across South Side neighborhoods, we could see a suppression effect on the overall numbers.
Translation: people organizing, people defending home, people refusing to be statistics. That's the Purge that nobody talks about.
Not the killing. The holding on. The barricades. The neighbors who decided to stand together.
Signal's stabilized. Jammer's either moved or burned out. We're solid for now.
See? We're still here. We're always still here.
Current time is {{CLOCK}}. We're {{ELAPSED}} into the Purge. 12 hours remaining until the all-clear.
Let me break down what we're expecting in the next hour specifically. Early-activity neighborhoods typically see opportunistic incidents first.
Single actors or small groups. Crimes of immediate impulse — robberies, assaults, settling old scores. Not organized yet.
Coordinated group action doesn't usually peak until Hours 3 and 4, when fatigue hasn't set in and adrenaline is still high.
So we're waiting for Hour 3 for the real chaos. Right now is the warm-up act.
Broadly speaking, yes. Though 'warm-up' is a grim way to describe it. But the pattern is consistent across five years of data.
Data. Right. We're reducing a night of complete lawlessness to a statistical pattern. That's how you survive this broadcast.
You treat it like weather. You treat it like sports scores. You treat it like anything except what it actually is.
But you're listening anyway. And we're talking anyway. So here we are.
I'm getting some scattered reports from listeners texting in. East Side is already lighting up. Sirens in the background of one caller.
No law enforcement, of course. They're protected government officials only. But it means activity. Holden was right about the first spike.
Early. But not unexpected. If we're seeing activity now, the pattern suggests we're 30 to 45 minutes ahead of 2168's Hour 1 curve.
Which means we should expect hour-end totals around 10 to 12, not 8. Still manageable as raw numbers.
But manageable is a word that loses meaning on Purge Night, doesn't it?
I'm adjusting the north antenna gain. If the jammer tries again, we'll have better resilience. But folks, if we do drop, we'll be back fast.
This is a one-night operation, and we're not losing this signal on my watch.
There's the confidence. Tim's been running this station through worse. He's going to get us through the night.
For those listening at home, I want to give you some baseline survival stuff, because that's part of why we're here.
One: stay inside. I know you're already inside. But stay there. Don't look out the window. Don't be tempted.
Two: if you barricade, barricade properly. Furniture against doors, windows covered, zero light inside that might attract attention.
Three: have water, have a phone charged, have a first aid kit. If something happens to someone in your household, you need to be ready.
Four: keep this radio on. Tuned to PRGE. We'll give you real-time updates. If an area is spiking, we'll tell you. If evacuation becomes necessary, Tim will broadcast routes.
Five: understand the statistical likelihood based on your neighborhood. East Side, elevated risk. Capitol Square, moderate risk. South Side with organized watch, reduced risk.
West Side wealth, extremely reduced risk. That's just mathematics. Not fair. Just mathematics.
And that's the thing nobody wants to say out loud but everybody knows: the Purge is worse if you're poor.
Rich people have security guards and panic rooms and lawyers. Everybody else has neighbors and prayers and us on the radio.
So we're here. We're going to be here all night. Every single hour, every single report, every single update.
North antenna is locked in. Signal's clean. We're ready for whatever the night throws at us.
{{ELAPSED}} in. The Purge is real. It's happening. And we're documenting it as it unfolds.
Next hour, we'll have detailed neighborhood breakdowns. We'll have weather updates as that cold front moves in. We'll have the real-time kill counts as Tucker gets them.
For now, I want to welcome everyone one more time. Thank you for staying home. Thank you for choosing this.
This is Purge Radio. We're your company for the next 12 hours. Don't turn us off. We've got a lot of night left.
Weather update coming at the top of the next hour. Right now: clear, 41 degrees, cold front incoming.
Stats update: early-activity neighborhoods confirm elevated Hour 1 projection. Expect 10 to 12 confirmed incidents by 20:00.
Broadcast is solid. Jammer is dormant. We're good to proceed through Hour 2.
Then we're on. We're rolling. The night is young and getting younger by the second.
This is Quinn Foley on PRGE. Stay safe out there. Or stay inside. Preferably both.
We'll talk again at 20:00. Twelve hours to dawn.

tickerItems.text: Madison-wide kill count at {{CLOCK}}: 3 confirmed. East Side leading. Consistent with early-activity baseline.
Signal interference ongoing. PRGE remains operational. Jammer source unknown, location north sector.
Capitol Square fortified. Perimeter secure. Private security alerts down from 2168 baseline — fewer attempted breaches so far.
Text-in report from Atwood Avenue resident: neighbors are barricaded, supplies stocked, waiting it out. Standard prep acknowledged.
